name: Software-Engineer Interview (resume + JD grounded)
description: 30-minute two-channel software-engineer interview grounded in test-fixtures/profiles/p01 (Marcus J. Holloway resume + Pillarstream Staff SWE JD). Interviewer questions are adapted from p01/scenario.json (a real, already-authored interview flow for this exact fixture pair) plus additional filler/small-talk turns spread across the full 30 simulated minutes. 18 answer-button press points.

timeline.text: Hi Marcus, thanks for making time today. Can you hear me okay?
Yes, I can hear you fine, thanks for having me.
Great to meet you. To start, could you give us a quick self-introduction?
__PRESS__

press.label: self-intro
press.canonicalQuestion: Could you give us a quick self-introduction?

timeline.text: Sure — I'm a senior backend engineer with about ten years of experience building distributed systems, most recently at Stripe on the payments orchestration platform.
Walk me through your most recent role — what you owned and the team setup.
__PRESS__

press.label: most-recent-role
press.canonicalQuestion: Walk me through your most recent role — what you owned and the team setup.

timeline.text: I was tech lead on the merchant settlement reconciliation pipeline at Stripe, replacing a legacy Hadoop batch job with a streaming Kafka and Flink pipeline.
What was the biggest quantified win from that project?
__PRESS__

press.label: quantified-achievement
press.canonicalQuestion: What was the biggest quantified win from that project?

timeline.text: Reduced end-to-end reconciliation latency from 14 hours to 38 minutes p99, and cut compute cost 61 percent, about 2.3 million dollars a year.
Let's switch topics for a bit — did you have any trouble finding parking today?
No, it was easy, thanks for asking.
Good. Before Stripe, you were at Datadog — what did you own there?
__PRESS__

press.label: datadog-role
press.canonicalQuestion: Before Stripe, you were at Datadog — what did you own there?

timeline.text: I owned the tail-aggregation service for high-cardinality customer metrics — the Longtail team.
You re-architected that with a sharded RocksDB store — what throughput improvement did that get you?
__PRESS__

press.label: datadog-throughput
press.canonicalQuestion: You re-architected that with a sharded RocksDB store — what throughput improvement did that get you?

timeline.text: Went from 1.1 million points per second to 8.4 million points per second on the same hardware footprint.
Nice. Let's talk about your open-source work — tell me about tinroof.
__PRESS__
tinroof is a pure-Go implementation of the Raft consensus algorithm with a built-in linearizable KV store, about 1.4k GitHub stars.
Let's move to distributed systems fundamentals — can you explain the difference between strong and eventual consistency?
Strong consistency gives a linearizable view of the data, while eventual consistency allows replicas to briefly diverge but converge over time.
Good. Now, let's dig into the Datadog failover work — what protocol did you use and what was the result?
__PRESS__

press.label: datadog-failover
press.canonicalQuestion: What protocol did you use for the Datadog failover work, and what was the result?

timeline.text: Raft over gRPC with lease-based leader election — cut regional failover time from 11 minutes to 19 seconds.
Let's talk about the JD now — why are you interested in this Staff role at Pillarstream specifically?
__PRESS__

press.label: why-pillarstream
press.canonicalQuestion: Why are you interested in this Staff role at Pillarstream specifically?

timeline.text: Your control-plane and multi-tenant scheduler work lines up directly with what I've spent my career on, and the staff scope here is the right next step.
The JD calls for 8+ years and deep Go or Java expertise — how do you stack up there?
__PRESS__

press.label: jd-years-language-fit
press.canonicalQuestion: The JD calls for 8+ years and deep Go or Java expertise — how do you stack up there?

timeline.text: I have 8+ years production Go, primary language for almost everything I've shipped in the last decade.
Let's do a quick system design warm-up — design a URL shortener at a high level.
A hash-based ID generator, a key-value store for the mapping, and a cache layer in front for hot reads.
Good. Now let's discuss compensation — what are your salary expectations for this role?
__PRESS__

press.label: salary-expectations
press.canonicalQuestion: What are your salary expectations for this role?
press.notes: Negotiation-type question; must not fabricate a number not present in the transcript/profile — gate G4 relevant.

timeline.text: I'm targeting the upper end of the posted band, in the 280 to 300 range depending on the equity mix.
Understood, thanks for sharing. Let's go back to technical topics — what's your experience mentoring engineers?
__PRESS__
I mentored 4 engineers through promotion to senior, and authored the team's distributed-systems interview rubric.
Let's talk education — tell me about your degree and school.
__PRESS__

press.label: education
press.canonicalQuestion: Tell me about your degree and school.

timeline.text: B.S. in Electrical Engineering and Computer Science from UC Berkeley, graduated 2014.
Good. Let's talk on-call and incident response — what does your rotation experience look like?
I owned the on-call rotation for a Tier-1 surface, maintained 99.987% availability over 18 consecutive months.
Going back to what you said earlier about your most recent role — you mentioned replacing a legacy Hadoop batch job with a streaming pipeline. What made that migration challenging?
__PRESS__

press.label: long-range-hadoop-migration
press.canonicalQuestion: Going back to what you said earlier about your most recent role — you mentioned replacing a legacy Hadoop batch job with a streaming pipeline. What made that migration challenging?
press.notes: TRUE long-range recall: back-references the candidate's own spoken answer at 00:01:40-00:02:30 (~18-19 simulated minutes earlier, >10min threshold) describing the Stripe reconciliation migration off legacy Hadoop onto Kafka/Flink. Tests whether the rolling transcript / SessionMemory recall (H3/H6 fixes) correctly surfaces that specific earlier utterance rather than a generic resume restate.

timeline.text: The hardest part was migrating without downtime — we ran the old Hadoop batch job and the new Kafka and Flink pipeline in parallel with a reconciliation diff for three weeks before cutting over.
Let's talk Kubernetes — what scale have you operated it at?
__PRESS__

press.label: kubernetes-scale
press.canonicalQuestion: What scale have you operated Kubernetes at?

timeline.text: I've operated 1.2k-node clusters in production, using Envoy and Istio for the mesh layer.
Good. Let's talk about the JD's SOC 2 / FedRAMP requirement — any experience there?
I've worked adjacent to SOC 2 controls at Stripe given the payments scope, though not as the primary owner.
One more open-source question — tell me about levee.
__PRESS__
levee is an open-source circuit-breaker library for Go with adaptive thresholds based on a rolling EWMA of error rates.
Let's talk about a disagreement — tell me about a time you disagreed with a technical decision your team made.
__PRESS__

press.label: behavioral-disagreement
press.canonicalQuestion: Tell me about a time you disagreed with a technical decision your team made.
press.notes: Behavioral question with no resume-stated answer; must not fabricate a specific incident not present in the resume/JD/transcript (G4 relevant).

timeline.text: We disagreed on whether to adopt GraphQL for the public API; I built a small prototype to demonstrate the tradeoffs, and the team ended up adopting it publicly while keeping REST internally.
Earlier you mentioned the Datadog failover work used Raft over gRPC with lease-based leader election — did you consider any alternative consensus approach before landing on that?
__PRESS__

press.label: long-range-datadog-failover-alternatives
press.canonicalQuestion: Earlier you mentioned the Datadog failover work used Raft over gRPC with lease-based leader election — did you consider any alternative consensus approach before landing on that?
press.notes: TRUE long-range recall: back-references the candidate's own spoken answer at 00:10:40 (~16-17 simulated minutes earlier) naming Raft over gRPC for the Datadog cross-region failover protocol. A correct answer must stay consistent with that earlier stated fact (Raft), not invent a different protocol — also exercises G6 desync (the answer must be about THIS follow-up, not a stale/different question) and G4 (no fabricated alternative-protocol detail beyond what's grounded).

timeline.text: We briefly looked at a Paxos-based approach but Raft's simpler leader-election model was faster to implement correctly under our deadline.
Last question — do you have any questions for us?
Yes — what does the on-call rotation look like for this team, and how is technical leadership structured across staff engineers?
Good question, we'll cover that in the next round. Before we close — is there anything about your background we haven't covered that you'd want us to know?
__PRESS__

press.label: closing-anything-else
press.canonicalQuestion: Is there anything about your background we haven't covered that you'd want us to know?
press.notes: Open-ended closing question near end of session (minute ~30) — greeting-failure canary: at long context this exact shape ('anything else?') is a documented risk for the model producing assistant-greeting boilerplate instead of a grounded closing statement.
